presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: My breathing has become much worse over the last few days
I'm coughing up more phlegm than usual and can't breathe properly
My inhalers aren't helping — I can't catch my breath
Worsening shortness of breath and a chesty cough
I can barely walk to the toilet without getting breathless

history_data.hpi: Patient with known COPD (GOLD stage III, on triple inhaler therapy) presents with a 3-day history of worsening dyspnoea, increased sputum production, and change in sputum colour from white to yellow-green. Symptoms began after an upper respiratory tract infection last week. Usual inhalers are providing minimal relief. Baseline exercise tolerance is approximately 100 metres on flat ground; now breathless at rest. Patient is on home oxygen 2L/min nocturnally but has been using it continuously today. No haemoptysis.
patient_quotes: I'm much more breathless than usual — I've never been this bad.
The phlegm has gone from white to a greenish colour.
Even walking to the bathroom leaves me struggling.
I started on a cold last week and it's gone straight to my chest.
pmh_pool: COPD — GOLD stage III, diagnosed 8 years ago
Heavy smoking history — 45 pack-years, quit 3 years ago
Ischaemic heart disease — previous NSTEMI 6 years ago, stented
Previous COPD hospitalisations — 2 admissions in past 2 years

social_history.smoking: Ex-smoker — quit 3 years ago, 45 pack-year history
social_history.exercise: Severely limited at baseline — walks approximately 100 metres on flat ground before stopping
social_history.stress: Moderate — lives alone, some social isolation

core_facts.sputum_change: increased volume and purulent — white to yellow-green

negative_history: No leg swelling or calf tenderness
No recent surgery or prolonged immobility
No new medications that could precipitate bronchospasm

general_appearance: Visibly breathless at rest — speaking in 2–3 word bursts
Using accessory muscles of respiration — sternocleidomastoid and intercostal recession
respiratory: Diffuse bilateral wheeze — expiratory and inspiratory
Hyper-resonant percussion bilaterally — consistent with hyperinflation

investigations.ABG (Arterial Blood Gas): pH 7.32 (acidaemia), PaO2 52 mmHg (hypoxaemia), PaCO2 62 mmHg (hypercapnia), HCO3 30 mmol/L (elevated — chronic compensation), BE +5. Pattern consistent with acute-on-chronic type 2 respiratory failure (hypercapnic respiratory failure). Bicarbonate elevation confirms chronic CO2 retention. Target SpO2 88–92% to avoid suppression of hypoxic drive.
investigations.Chest X-Ray: Bilateral hyperinflation with flattened hemidiaphragms. Increased anteroposterior diameter. No focal consolidation. No pneumothorax. No pleural effusion. Appearances consistent with severe COPD — no new acute parenchymal change identified.
investigations.ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 108 bpm. Right axis deviation. P pulmonale — tall peaked P waves in lead II, consistent with chronic cor pulmonale. No acute ST changes or features of right heart strain acutely.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 13.6 × 10⁹/L (elevated — consistent with bacterial infection), Neutrophils 11.2 × 10⁹/L (neutrophilia), Hb 16.8 g/dL (polycythaemia — chronic hypoxia), Haematocrit 0.52, Platelets 312 × 10⁹/L. Polycythaemia consistent with long-standing hypoxaemia.
investigations.CRP: 78 mg/L — significantly elevated, consistent with active infection as precipitant of this exacerbation.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 136 mmol/L, K 3.4 mmol/L (mildly low — monitor with salbutamol nebulisations), Creatinine 102 µmol/L, Urea 7.8 mmol/L, eGFR 62 — mildly impaired baseline renal function.
investigations.Sputum Culture: Sent for MC&S — results pending. Empirical antibiotic therapy initiated based on clinical picture of purulent sputum and raised inflammatory markers. Common pathogens in COPD exacerbation: Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Moraxella catarrhalis.
investigations.Peak Flow: Unable to perform reliably due to severity of exacerbation. Peak flow is less useful in COPD compared to asthma — spirometry (FEV1/FVC ratio) is the gold standard for COPD diagnosis and staging, but not performed acutely.

hidden_diagnosis: Acute Exacerbation of COPD — Infective (Type 2 Respiratory Failure)

patient_rules.emotional_state: breathless and fatigued — too breathless to speak in full sentences, visibly exhausted

essential_questions: onset and duration of worsening breathlessness
change from baseline — usual exercise tolerance vs current
fever or systemic features of infection
smoking history — pack-years, current or ex-smoker
known COPD — GOLD stage, previous spirometry
previous exacerbations — number per year, previous hospitalisations
previous ICU admissions or NIV use
home oxygen — flow rate, hours per day
any new medications (beta-blockers, sedatives) that could precipitate
leg swelling or calf pain — exclude PE
essential_examinations: general assessment — accessory muscle use, pursed-lip breathing, ability to speak
oxygen saturation — on air and on current oxygen
cardiovascular — signs of cor pulmonale: raised JVP, peripheral oedema
critical_actions: recognise acute exacerbation of COPD and identify precipitant (infective vs non-infective)
assess for type 2 respiratory failure — obtain ABG; do NOT rely on SpO2 alone in known CO2 retainer
controlled oxygen therapy: target SpO2 88–92% — avoid high-flow oxygen which can suppress hypoxic drive and worsen hypercapnia
salbutamol 2.5–5mg nebulised with air (NOT oxygen unless SpO2 <88%) — repeat every 4–6 hours
ipratropium bromide 0.5mg nebulised — add in moderate-severe exacerbation
systemic corticosteroids: prednisolone 30–40mg oral daily for 5 days
antibiotics if purulent sputum or clinical evidence of infection: amoxicillin 500mg TDS or doxycycline 200mg loading then 100mg daily for 5 days
send sputum culture before starting antibiotics
consider non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP) if pH <7.35 and PaCO2 >6 kPa despite initial treatment — reduces need for intubation and mortality
monitor potassium — hypokalaemia with repeated salbutamol; replace if <3.0 mmol/L
thromboprophylaxis — enoxaparin if admitted; COPD patients at increased VTE risk
escalate to ICU if NIV fails or pH continues to fall
smoking cessation reinforcement even in ex-smokers
early physiotherapy and pulmonary rehabilitation referral post-discharge
red_flags: pH <7.35 with elevated PaCO2 — acute type 2 respiratory failure requiring NIV consideration
pH <7.25 — impending respiratory arrest; escalate to ICU
Inability to speak more than 1–2 words
Reduced conscious level or confusion — CO2 narcosis
Silent chest — minimal air entry despite distress
Previous ICU admission or intubation for COPD
Failure to respond to initial bronchodilator therapy
acceptable_differentials: Acute asthma exacerbation — younger patient, atopic history, fully reversible; but can coexist with COPD (ACOS)
Community-acquired pneumonia — consolidation on CXR, higher fever; can precipitate COPD exacerbation
Acute decompensated heart failure — cardiac wheeze, bilateral crackles, elevated BNP, cardiomegaly
Pulmonary embolism — sudden onset, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, risk factors; no purulent sputum
Pneumothorax — sudden deterioration, reduced air entry unilaterally, hyperresonance unilaterally
management_actions: Controlled oxygen: 24–28% via Venturi mask — target SpO2 88–92%
Salbutamol 2.5–5mg nebulised with air — every 4–6 hours
Amoxicillin 500mg TDS or doxycycline 200mg loading then 100mg daily — 5 days (if purulent sputum or infection suspected)
ABG on air then repeat after 1 hour of treatment — monitor pH and PaCO2 trend
NIV (BiPAP) if pH <7.35 with hypercapnia despite initial treatment
VTE prophylaxis with low molecular weight heparin
Chest X-Ray to exclude pneumothorax and pneumonia
Monitor blood glucose — steroids can cause hyperglycaemia
Review and optimise inhaler regimen before discharge
critical_history_questions: When did your breathing get worse compared to your usual?
What is your normal exercise tolerance — how far can you usually walk?
Has your phlegm changed — more of it? Changed colour?
Have you had a cold or infection recently?
How many times have you been admitted to hospital with your breathing in the past year?
Have you ever needed a breathing machine (NIV or ventilator) before?
Are you on home oxygen? How many litres and for how long each day?
Have you been using your inhalers regularly?
Do you still smoke, or when did you quit?
Any leg swelling or calf pain?
Have you taken any new medications recently — including beta-blockers or sleeping tablets?
Do you know what stage your COPD is — have you had breathing tests (spirometry)?
